claim: YOLO provider dispatch API/state handling does not drift between Claude, Codex, and custom provider assumptions in demand evidence mode. The provider-adapter and evidence-dispatch implementations maintain provider-specific isolation while preserving full tool capability under harness boundary.

evidence.summary: buildProviderInvocation function implements separate code paths for each provider type (codex, custom, claude) with provider-specific argument construction and no shared state leakage between providers.
evidence.why: This is the core dispatch logic that ensures provider-specific isolation; each provider has its own branch with dedicated config handling.

evidence.summary: Codex provider uses exec --cd --sandbox --approval-policy --output-last-message with codex_model override, completely separate from Claude argument patterns.
evidence.why: Demonstrates provider isolation: codex has its own execution model and output capture mechanism that doesn't leak into claude or custom providers.

evidence.summary: Custom provider uses sh -c with rendered command template, isolated from codex and claude argument patterns.
evidence.why: Shows custom provider has its own execution path that doesn't inherit assumptions from other providers.

evidence.summary: Claude provider uses -p --model --permission-mode --settings --tools --allowedTools --disallowedTools --disable-slash-commands --no-session-persistence --max-budget-usd, with no leakage from codex or custom provider patterns.
evidence.why: Demonstrates claude-specific argument construction that is isolated from other provider implementations.

evidence.summary: spawnProviderPrompt function is provider-agnostic: it delegates to buildProviderInvocation and handles stdout/stderr capture uniformly across providers without assuming provider-specific behavior.
evidence.why: This shows the API boundary: spawnProviderPrompt doesn't embed provider-specific assumptions; it delegates to provider-specific logic.

evidence.summary: executionConfig function normalizes provider-specific settings: explicit provider/model selection, custom_command handling, and agent_tool_profile isolation per provider type.
evidence.why: Demonstrates evidence dispatch maintains provider separation at configuration time, preventing cross-provider contamination.

evidence.summary: executionConfig explicitly prevents codex from inheriting claude default model: if provider is codex and model contains 'claude', the model field is cleared to avoid cross-provider model leakage.
evidence.why: This is explicit protection against cross-provider model leakage, directly addressing the demand concern.

evidence.summary: Claude provider configuration normalizes agent_tool_profile into three modes (research, boundary, full) and clears settings/tools flags to rely on boundary enforcement rather than tool restrictions.
evidence.why: Shows that claude evidence mode preserves full tool capability while using agent_tool_profile for semantic labeling, not tool restriction.

evidence.summary: Custom provider sets custom_sandbox to 'boundary' for demand evidence mode, isolating custom agent execution under the same boundary contract.
evidence.why: Demonstrates that custom provider is also subject to boundary enforcement in evidence mode.

evidence.summary: buildBoundarySnapshot uses git ls-files and filesystem walking to create a SHA256 digest snapshot of all project files before agent execution, with allowed_write_roots exclusion.
evidence.why: This is the core boundary enforcement mechanism that applies equally to all providers.

evidence.summary: diffBoundarySnapshots compares before/after digests to detect any project mutations (added, modified, deleted files) regardless of which provider was used.
evidence.why: Shows boundary violation detection is provider-agnostic, catching mutations from any provider.

evidence.summary: runDemandEvidenceDispatchRuntime executes evidence agents with boundary snapshots before/after, then runs boundary diff and converts any changes into BOUNDARY_PROJECT_MUTATION blockers in readiness.
evidence.why: Demonstrates the complete boundary enforcement workflow that applies to all providers equally.

evidence.summary: Boundary violations are added directly to readiness blockers, preventing prd_ready when any provider violates the boundary.
evidence.why: Shows that boundary violations from any provider become hard blockers that prevent PRD readiness.

evidence.summary: Test 'codex dispatch does not inherit a claude default model or force read-only sandbox' verifies codex provider isolation and that provider-specific config doesn't leak from other providers.
evidence.why: Executable test evidence confirming cross-provider leakage protection.

evidence.summary: Test 'claude dispatch does not pass tool restriction flags and relies on boundary enforcement' confirms claude evidence mode preserves full tool capability under boundary protection.
evidence.why: Test evidence that tool capability is preserved while boundary provides safety instead of tool restrictions.

evidence.summary: Test 'claude full tool profile requires explicit authorization for full semantic mode' verifies agent_tool_profile isolation and explicit authorization gating.
evidence.why: Test evidence showing tool profile modes are isolated and require explicit authorization.

evidence.summary: Test 'boundary blocks project file mutation even when agent results pass' confirms boundary enforcement works regardless of provider or agent result quality.
evidence.why: Test evidence that boundary is provider-agnostic and catches mutations from any source.

evidence.summary: Test 'git-backed boundary detects untracked project mutations' verifies boundary works with git repositories and catches mutations from any provider.
evidence.why: Test evidence showing boundary enforcement integrates with git and is provider-independent.

assumptions: The git status showing modified files represents the current working state of the demand evidence system
Claude Code CLI '-p' flag behavior matches the provider-adapter implementation assumptions
Codex CLI '--output-last-message' flag behavior is consistent across versions
risks: If Claude Code changes its '-p' flag behavior or tool permission model, the boundary enforcement might need updates
If Codex changes its output-last-message behavior, the evidence extraction logic might break
Custom provider commands that mutate project files outside the allowed_write_roots would be caught by boundary but might cause confusion

result.notes: Provider dispatch API/state handling is properly isolated between Claude, Codex, and custom providers. Each provider has its own execution path in buildProviderInvocation. executionConfig explicitly prevents cross-provider model leakage. Boundary enforcement via buildBoundarySnapshot/diffBoundarySnapshots applies equally to all providers and converts violations into readiness blockers. Test coverage confirms isolation, tool capability preservation, and boundary enforcement across providers.
